data.title: SHYG: Less Upside From Compression
data.description:
Introduction & Thesis

In my last post on the iShares 0-5 Year High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (SHYG), I mentioned that the "hold" investment thesis for a six-month horizon was supported by a normalization of credit spreads (300 bp). On this occasion, the analytical framework remains unchanged. What has changed is the evolution of credit spreads, as the spread is currently around 240 bp.

This reduces the additional compression margin of the OAS itself and means that the expected return for the medium to long term will be more dominated by the ETF's carry. In this context, the relevant risk is not so much duration (which is low), but rather a cyclical episode of widening. These types of episodes occur when the market demands a higher risk premium. If that happens, the price of the ETF falls rapidly so that the carry takes time to offset that fall. Therefore, today's thesis remains to hold and buy only during episodes of spread widening, when the risk premium returns to "paying the tail" of credit.

To identify an episode of widening in credit spreads, we will have to see that the market is indeed beginning to pay more premiums for assumed risk. This can be clearly seen by monitoring Treasury bonds and credit spreads, which we will analyze later. To help the reader understand this better, carry is currently at acceptable levels, but there is a certain asymmetry between potential gains and losses at this time.

In terms of price, the upside potential is somewhat limited by the compression of credit spreads (240 bp). On the other hand, the downside potential is greater in terms of probability if a significant macroeconomic event occurs. In my view, the credit spread could compress somewhat further (50-60 bp), which could provide the fund with an extra 1-1.2% return, apart from the carry, but if a macroeconomic shock occurs, it would be quite feasible to see spreads widen by 200-300 bp. This means that the fund's price would fall by approximately 4%, which is why I continue to recommend holding and only buying when such a situation occurs.

ETF Overview

Analyzing the main characteristics of the ETF, we see what we discussed in the first section. Currently, the ETF provides a 30-day security yield of 6.24%, which is very decent in terms of carry. The effective duration is 1.89 years, which indicates that it is a fund that is not very sensitive to interest rate movements, and the OAS of corporate credit is 240.72 basis points. It has 1,148 securities in its portfolio and a 12-month trailing yield of 7.01%, which also speaks well for the consistency of the carry paid by the fund. Finally, it has annual associated expenses of 0.30%, as measured by the expense ratio published on its website.

The Portfolio

At the sector level, its holdings are distributed with a weighting of 19.21% in the cyclical consumer sector, followed by 14.07% in the communications sector and 12.46% in the non-cyclical consumer sector. As can be seen, at the sector level, the portfolio is constructed to achieve a relatively good balance between cyclical and defensive sectors.

The maturity structure of the bonds is organized as follows: bonds with maturities between 3 and 5 years (medium-term bonds) lead with 38.55%, followed by bonds with maturities between 0 and 1 year with a weight of 23.23% (ultra-short-term bonds). The 2-3 year maturity tranche represents 21.52%, and the 1-2 year tranche represents 14.80%.

The credit quality rating reflects, as the reader may have guessed, the type of asset to which this ETF is exposed. They buy sub-investment-grade and high-yield corporate bonds, which is why they offer such a competitive yield (carry). The fund is passively managed, seeking to replicate the performance of the Markit iBoxx USD Liquid High Yield 0-5 Index before fees. Currently, 48.94% is invested in corporate bonds with a BB credit rating, 33.47% in bonds with a B rating, and 13.66% in high-yield bonds with a CCC rating.

Payoff Profile

In terms of carry, the fund has effectively paid 7% over the last 12 months, according to data provided by Seeking Alpha. Dividend growth over the same period is 2.07%, partly due to falling interest rates. The last two items in the table also show positive data. The ETF has been paying a growing dividend for four years, which coincides with the monetary policy cycle imposed by the Federal Reserve in recent years, and has been paying dividends for 12 consecutive years, which tells us about its recurrence in carry payments and its usefulness as a tool for generating consistent income.

Macro Drivers

This last section helps us see what is happening with the drivers that influence the carry and the price of the fund. The first image shows us the evolution of the credit spread compared to the S&P 500, which allows us to see where equities stand in relation to this spread. The current situation is clearly risk-on, which confirms the previous statement that the market is paying less for the risk assumed. The second image shows us the curve regime between the 2-5 Y segment, which is where the fund currently stands. The colors indicate different curve regimes, classified as bear, bull, steeper, or flattener. Today, the 2-5 Y term spread has turned positive and is in a steepener twist regime, which means that recent credit movements have been somewhat more linked to the dynamics of interest rate cuts. This is why I maintain my hold thesis, with a call option in situations of widening. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Kraft Heinz: Berkshire May Be Out Of The Equation And What That Means For Shareholders
data.description:
Headaches for Kraft Heinz (KHC) shareholders keep on piling up as just very recently a regulatory filing revealed that the largest shareholder of the company - Berkshire Hathaway, Inc (BRK.B) intends to sell a part of the full amount of its stake within the company.

For those who have been following recent events surrounding the upcoming split of the company, including the rather unusual criticism by Buffett and his successor, the intention to sell is probably not a surprise.

The market, however, was caught off-guard by the regulatory filing and KHC share price reacted accordingly - falling by almost 6% on the following day.

As a result, KHC is now down 24% over the past year and almost 10% since my last warning that investors are better-off waiting for the split before taking action.

The fact that yesterday's share price movement was caused by a significant spike in trading volume that is well-above the 30-day average (41m against 15m) is a sign that share price volatility will likely persist in the coming days or even weeks. Speculations about the next move by the Oracle of Omaha could further exacerbate this problem even before any potential selling begins.

More Selling Pressure

The filing itself does not mean that Berkshire Hathaway intends to sell its entire stake in KHC, but the fact that it owns 27.5% of the company could have serious consequences for future returns, even if BRK.B trims a small portion of its stake.

A stake of this size would most likely be reduced over a number of quarters if not an entire year in order to avoid tanking the share price. Prior examples when BRK.B sold sizable amounts of shares in even larger business show that such transactions are carried out over multiple quarter and this puts consistent downward pressure to prices.

Bank of America (BAC) is one of the most recent examples. BRK.B's stake in the company was roughly 13% as of the end of Q2 2024. At that time BAC was trading at $40 apiece. In each of the following quarters, BRK.B went on sell BAC shares in each of the following quarters, up until the last reported one - Q3 of 2025. Although BAC is now trading 30% higher, the share price was trading sideways for many months following the first 13F release by BRK.B.

More importantly, the selling pressure was front-loaded during Q3 of 2024 which helps explain the share price volatility in that period and also why BAC significantly underperformed the sector in that quarter (see the second graph below).

Oracle (ORCL) is another example where BRK.B sold more than 40m shares in a single three-month period - during Q4 of 2018. The stake was much lower and the 40m shares represented around 1% ownership. The selling, however, coincided with a broader bear market in equities during the last quarter of 2018 so the overall impact is much harder to quantify. On top of that ORCL had a sizable share buyback program to the tune of $500m a year at that time which helped offset the selling pressure.

This brings the question of share buybacks and their role in cushioning the potential impact from a large shareholder selling activities. Kraft Heinz recently introduced share buyback program is roughly $900m per annum, while the current stake of BRK.B in the company is valued at around $7.8bn. Thus buybacks would definitely help, but will be insufficient to fully offset a potential exit by the Oracle of Omaha.

The Playbook

As I outlined back in November of last year, the best course of action when it comes to KHC is to wait for the company split in the second half of 2026. I still believe that BRK.B would most likely stick with the Global Taste Elevation Company which is also the better pick of the two, but all that is initial speculations and it will be best to await for the full information to become available after the split is complete.

When it comes to the recent regulatory filing by BRK.B, it doesn't change my thesis as any selling activity could be done in preparation for the split. More likely than not, however, we could see Berkshire beginning to trim its positions in Q1 of 2026 which based on the examples seen above would put pressure on the KHC share price. This once again confirms my view that the best approach for now is to wait and see.

data.title: Albertsons: Is This Stock Too Good To Be True?
data.description:
Albertsons (ACI) is a company that has never really interested me much. After all the confusion surrounding the failed merger with Kroger (KR), it seemed like a company without much appeal, lacking clear direction, and neither sufficiently asymmetric in valuation nor compelling enough in terms of quality to be considered an alternative buy to Costco (COST), Walmart (WMT), or even Kroger.

But I decided to revisit the case and listen to the Q3 earnings call, and what I saw ended up opening my eyes a little. The timing is still not good, and there will certainly still be challenges, but management is addressing the problems and the valuation is currently very attractive, by far the best among its major peers.

Therefore, even though there are uncertainties, I don't think this is a case of “too good to be true.” It doesn't seem to be a value trap, but rather a value play.

Albertsons: The Bad News First

Albertsons' Q3 earnings alone were not very encouraging. Management even called this quarter “solid,” with revenue increasing ~2% YoY. But even this low single-digit performance is not that encouraging, because it was driven by a reduction in the number of stores, in addition to lower fuel sales, which, although it may seem temporary, it is difficult to be very clear whether this is just a brief optimization of the portfolio or whether it stems from a more structural problem that will impact future expansion.

The margin was also not so good, with gross margin falling from 27.9% to 27.4%, and even removing some effects such as fuel, gross margin still fell by 0.5 percentage points due to the higher share of delivery and other higher costs. The good news is that SG&A decreased in relation to revenue due to operating leverage.

During the Q3 earnings call, much was said about inflation and other macroeconomic issues, attributing this more modest result to a difficult landscape, along with the company's initiatives to be more price efficient, as in the excerpt below:

“We're working very hard to soften the pass-through of inflation to our customers. So that said, we are pleased with the progress that we see in our price investments to date.”

However, this also has a competitive aspect. The excerpt above was from management responding to one of the analysts present at the earnings call who mentioned that other competitors are also making these price investments, and this reflects the current scenario well. The peers mentioned, Costco, Walmart, Kroger, all have a larger scale and can better absorb costs. For example, Costco's very low gross margin, which is deliberately lower than that of its peers, is much debated in the market.

But note two points in the table below. The first is that ACI's gross margin is the highest among the peers I mentioned earlier, which, despite being a positive point for the business they have structured, is also a point of attention, as there is more room to decrease and enter the average of this competitive landscape. A second point is efficiency: even with lower margins, all other players deliver higher EBITDA and EBIT margins.

Furthermore, considering this structure, we have companies such as Costco and Walmart expanding. Companies such as Amazon (AMZN) and Mercado Libre (MELI) are entering the grocery delivery market more aggressively, and all of this increases uncertainty about the future. If Albertsons is currently struggling to stand out in the market, in 10 years' time, there may be even greater pressure. Think locally: if Albertsons is stronger in a certain region today, that may change in a few years if the company continues to decrease or stabilize its number of stores while its competitors are more aggressive. And even though we have some level of loyalty with millions of loyalty members, this “stickiness” is not as high as that of a Costco.

Another point that is not so interesting in the case today is the debt in relation to free cash flow. Net debt is at $15.2 billion, which seems manageable, but in the first nine months of 2025, operating cash flow was $1.6 billion, and CapEx was over $1.4 billion, meaning free cash flow was quite compressed. So even though the capital structure is not super concerning, it is another yellow flag.

Albertsons Is Solving These Problems

The good part is that I heard exactly what I wanted to hear on the earnings call. Even with a low valuation, allowing for a high shareholder yield, the sentiment was that the number one priority is not valuation (although it is one of the priorities), but rather the transformation and restructuring of the business.

This connects to the previous point about free cash flow being pressured by still-high CapEx, even with the number of stores decreasing, precisely because Albertsons is investing heavily in several areas. One of the points frequently mentioned by management recently was investments in tech, such as what they called “AI transformation,” which were made to improve Albertsons' digital side, and the company is already reaping the rewards. It was mentioned that “Ask AI” feature increased customer basket size by about 10%, and in addition, one of the points that “saved” Q3 was digital sales, which grew 21%, and loyalty members, which increased 12% YoY.

AI also helps with the pricing issue I mentioned in the section above. Competition is a factor that drives prices down, increasing price wars, but with investment in tech, the company is able to do this much more accurately, so I see this as a positive.

In addition, physical infrastructure, such as the 23 remodels carried out in Q3, is another factor that significantly increases CapEx, not to mention other factors such as investment to increase private labels.

What I mean here is that the case is not as simple as “Albertsons is losing ground... and free cash flow is under pressure.” It's more like, yes, Albertsons is not in a good place right now, and free cash flow is a little under pressure because the company is investing heavily to remain competitive.

Then... Shareholder Value Remains

It seems quite feasible to me that in a few years, let's say in 2030, these initiatives will be much more mature, with new stores providing a more acceptable return, consolidated margins increasing due to increased efficiency in AI, and revenue growth at a healthier level with potential improvements in pricing.

It is still a case full of uncertainties, and we need to see very disciplined execution by management, but still, it seems very feasible to me that after the strategies mature and this more turbulent time passes, there will be many benefits for shareholders.

Think about it with me. Today, the P/E ratio is 8x. Kroger, which is the second lowest multiple, is at 13x. I don't think there will be a rerating to a “Walmart-like” level, with ACI trading at 30x earnings or more, but I think it's feasible to think that after getting back on track, the market will see potential and the company will trade at something closer to 15x.

Another point worth considering is future EPS. The market itself already estimates a recovery for fiscal year 2027, with a slight improvement in business and buybacks, with estimates at $2.30. Today, ACI stock is trading at $17. We are saying that if the company distributes all of this in dividends/buybacks, the yield is 13%. But don't consider this the base scenario, because there is still a lot to be done.

However, a scenario that is not so impossible is for EPS to be close to ~$2.60 in 2029 or 2030, with initiatives already well mature, and the company choosing to distribute 50% or 60% of this, which would still give a high single-digit yield.

The Bottom Line

Well, I started the analysis with the question of whether Albertsons' case was too good to be true, and I don't think that's really the case. It's an honest investment thesis. There are uncertainties, there will be significant challenges, but at the same time, these risks do not make the case unfeasible. In fact, with this valuation at such a depressed level, there is a huge margin of safety.

Therefore, I think the current risk reward is attractive, and my rating for ACI stock is a buy.

data.title: Northern Trust: Strong Stock Markets Continue To Pad Earnings
data.description:
Rampant global stock markets haven’t just been great for investors’ portfolios; they have also been a boon for custody banks’ earnings. Chicago's Northern Trust (NTRS) hasn't missed out, with buoyant asset-based fees helping the bank's earnings per share up nearly 20% last year. As you would expect, that has been rocket fuel for Northern's stock. Shares have returned nearly 40% this past year, more than 20 points better than the S&P 500.

Writing about the company back in October, I did say that my only major problem was the valuation. With the shares up around 20% since then, Northern has only gotten more expensive, leaving its price-to-book ratio well beyond its historical average. With that partly resting on historically expensive stock markets, I would wait for a pullback before buying.

Strong Operating Leverage

Global stock markets delivered strong performances last year. While international equities led the way, the S&P 500 still returned around 18%, marking its third straight year of double-digit gains.

With around half of its custodied assets sitting in equities, this has been a boon for Northern's asset-based servicing revenue. The bank booked $496.4 million in custody & fund administration fees last quarter, up 9% year-on-year.

Of course, Northern is more than just a custody bank. It is also a global wealth and investment manager. Equities accounted for over 55% of Northern’s assets under management last quarter, meaning buoyant stock markets have also been a tailwind for this part of its business. Indeed, Northern generated $578 million in Wealth fees last quarter, up 6% year-on-year.

Asset-based revenue isn't the only thing that’s been strong for Northern recently. Net interest income, which was around 30% of the bank’s revenue last quarter, has also been at a cyclically strong level. Like many banks, Northern has benefited from cheaper funding costs and fixed-asset repricing in its bond portfolio. Its overall asset yield is still falling in the face of lower short-term interest rates, but it has lost less from this process than it has gained from lower borrowing yields.

As a result, net interest income grew a strong 10% sequentially last quarter, with Northern’s net interest margin expanding by 11 basis points to 1.81%. That is the widest it has been in over a decade.

Because there aren't really any incremental expenses associated with these trends, they have been great for operating leverage. Northern's adjusted revenue growth outpaced underlying cost growth by 400 basis points last quarter – one reason why its earnings growth has been so impressive of late.

I would make a similar point regarding capital and profitability. Because its earnings growth is coming from capital-light activity (higher asset-based fee revenue and an expanding net interest margin), it has been highly accretive to Northern's return on equity. The bank recorded a 15.4% return on equity last quarter, which was roughly 200 basis points above its prior 5-year average.

Soaring Shareholder Payouts

Good news isn’t limited to the income statement. Northern’s balance sheet is also in a strong position right now, with the bank sitting on a 12.6% Common Equity Tier 1 ratio at the end of 2025. This is comfortably ahead of management’s 11-12% target range.

Coupled with its strong profit growth, high ROE, and relatively low reinvestment needs, this is supporting excellent capital returns to its common stockholders. Northern spent just under $1.9 billion on dividends and buybacks last year, around 25% more than in 2024. Of course, the buyback component is helping the bank's share-level bottom line. Average diluted shares fell 5% in 2025 - a second reason why Northern's earnings growth has been so impressive.

Initial 2026 guidance implies this may continue. While operating leverage is seen moderating sharply, management still thinks it can be over 100 basis points this year, while the bank's payout ratio is seen above 100%. Given Northern's current market value is around $27 billion, investors are looking at an attractive high single-digit forward shareholder yield.

Premium Valuation

While everything looks great for Northern right now, the main issue is that this is reflected in its valuation. Its stock trades for $150 as I type, which is up from around $108 a year ago. That move has seen Northern’s price-to-book multiple expand by over 30% in that time. At just over 2.3 times, it’s now running at a circa 20% premium to its 10-year average.

Northern has grown its book value at a circa 6% annualized rate over that period. It also offers a 2.1% dividend yield. Together, that creates a reasonable expectation of an 8%-9% long-term annualized return. My main concern is this: with Northern’s price-to-book ratio at a premium to its past average, mean reversion would be a headwind to equity returns, eating into whatever Northern delivers from book value growth and dividend.

Now, Northern’s premium price-to-book multiple reflects the fact that its return on equity is strong right now. However, this is resting to a degree on elevated equity markets. As you can see in the chart above, valuations for the S&P 500 look stretched by historical standards. Any correction would weigh on Northern’s ability to generate positive operating leverage, its ROE, and its current level of capital returns. That would likely have a negative knock-on impact on the stock. For that reason, I would like to see Northern drop below its long-run price-to-book ratio before being a buyer. Accordingly, I continue to rate shares "Hold."

data.title: TOYO: Made In USA Scale And Ethiopia Cost Edge Make The Stock A Strong Buy
data.description:
When I first wrote about TOYO Co., Ltd. (TOYO), I showed that this vertically integrated solar manufacturer had a history of stock market outperformance since its July 2024 IPO, thanks to rapid expansion and strategic acquisitions. The scaling of its cost-advantaged Ethiopia plant, the redirecting of Vietnam capacity toward non-U.S. markets, and the ramping up of its U.S. module production to benefit from "made-in-USA" demand and incentives, were sources of strength. Although margins and growth had softened around the time of my thesis due to ramp-up costs and shifting market mix, commentary from management suggested that there would be a rebound as new capacity and the VSUN brand integration matured. With the company trading at an attractive valuation compared to its peers, there was significant upside assuming profitability bounced back. This made the stock a "Strong Buy" for me. TOYO only files half-year and annual reports, so there have been no updates to the company's financial results since my first article. However, there have been developments regarding its U.S. operations and the integration of its VSUN brand that reinforce my original rating.

Market-Beating Stock Performance

Since that first article in September of last year, the stock has gone up 7.35% compared to total shareholder returns ("TSR") of 4.62% for the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and 5.01% for the iShares Russell 3000 ETF (IWV). This continues the company's history as a market-beater.

U.S. Plant Moves to Commercial Production

I noted in that first article that TOYO's November 2024 acquisition of Solar Plus Technology Texas LLC to bolster its U.S. presence brought with it a "567,140 sq. ft. Houston-area site with an initial 2.5 GW solar module capacity, expandable to 6.5 GW by 2029". Phase 1 of the project was completed in early 2025, with management projecting 1GW of solar output by mid-2025, which would scale to 2.5GW by the end of 2025. Trial production commenced upon acquisition. In October, management announced that it had begun commercial production, saying that,

"During the trial production phase, the Company has received strong demand from its U.S. clients for the modules manufactured at the Houston Facility. As operations scale, TOYO anticipates robust demand from utility-scale developers seeking reliable and domestically manufactured modules that qualify for favorable tax incentives."

Moreover, the company's chairman and CEO, Junsei Ryu, reiterated that commercial production was "a major step forward in our 'made-in-USA-for-the-USA' strategy". Production in the U.S., is, according to management,

"expected to qualify for tax incentives under Section 45X of the Internal Revenue Code, providing up to $0.07 per watt in production credits through 2030. This strengthens TOYO's cost competitiveness while supporting broader goals for domestic energy manufacturing."

It needs reiterating that solar manufacturing is dominated by the Chinese, whose economies of scale are unmatched. The Production Tax Credit (PTC) for solar energy gives producers a base credit of $0.0275 per kilowatt-hour for solar electricity they generate. By subsidizing domestic production rather than installation, the incentives directly improve TOYO's unit economics while creating a sustained competitive advantage against imported modules that cannot access similar benefits. Furthermore, a side effect of changes to the PTC in the One Big Beautiful Bill ("OBBB) Act is that they give existing producers a competitive advantage over market entrants. Under those changes, solar producers must have been placed in service by December 31, 2027, or construction must have begun by July 4, 2026.

Management continues to believe that the combination of robust domestic demand and constrained domestic supply, presents a unique opportunity. Unsaid in this is that the combination of the two supports higher prices and therefore TOYO's efforts to improve its profitability.

TOYO Has Further Deepened Its Ties With the U.S.

In addition, management plans on establishing a R&D center in the United States, further building out its supply chain in the country. In early December last year, TOYO announced that it had agreed to procure critical raw materials from a U.S. polysilicon supplier, further aligning its supply chain with American policy priorities. In December 2025, TOYO acquired the remaining stake in TOYO Solar LLC, its American subsidiary which was already engaged in advanced development of a 2.5 GW solar module manufacturing facility. In the words of chairman and CEO Junsei Ryu,

"this acquisition marks a key milestone in our U.S. growth strategy. Full ownership of TOYO Solar LLC strengthens our operational control, aligns our long-term interests, and reinforces our commitment to delivering American-made solar solutions at scale."

These moves serve to create a more robust and capable supply chain and aid in TOYO's Made-in-America plans, ensuring shorter delivery cycles and reduced logistics costs. Collectively, these features make TOYO more competitive. The presence of TOYO across different segments of the market and American value chain adds gravitas to this position.

Management Projects a Record 2025

In a November 2025 investor deck, management projected an FY2025 with its biggest solar cell shipments and net income levels ever. On the back of solar cell shipments rising from 1.74 gigawatts ("GW") in 2024 to between 4.2 GW and 4.4 GW in 2025, management expects revenue to reach between $375 million and $400 million, and net income to leap from $5.4 million in 2024 to between $39 million to $45 million in 2025. Importantly, these estimates do not take into account contributions from the U.S. facility, so are conservative.

Ethiopia is Driving Growth

Driving growth in solar cell shipments and profitability is the firm's N-Type cell line 4 GW annual capacity plant in Hawass, Ethiopia. Since the April 2025 installation of the 2 GW facility, production has now reached the full allocation of 4 GW. Just as with the U.S., TOYO is able to benefit from favorable investment policies, as well as the country's competitive power rates and abundant supply of hydropower. The Ethiopian plant has orders for the whole of the year, providing shipment visibility.

The Ethiopian plant can serve the U.S. market, given that Ethiopia is subject to a 10% tariff and does not fall under any anti-dumping or countervailing duties. The scale of the facility gives TOYO the ability to respond aggressively to new demand around the world, while benefitting from a low-cost region.

With TOYO's Vietnam operations now facing constrained U.S. market access, the Ethiopian capacity serves as both a production hedge and a market access vehicle, allowing the company to redirect its Vietnam output to non-U.S. markets while serving American customers from Ethiopia.

VSUN Acquisition Extending Integrated Value Chain

The VSUN brand acquisition has proven instrumental in TOYO's market penetration strategy. VSUN's established relationships with U.S. utility-scale developers and its reputation for quality have provided TOYO with immediate credibility in the American market. This brand recognition complements TOYO's manufacturing capabilities, allowing the company to leverage existing customer relationships while introducing its cost-advantaged production methods. The integration has streamlined go-to-market efforts significantly.

Policy-Driven Market Segmentation Continues

As Chinese producers continue expanding capacity despite softening demand growth rates, there is a supply glut that benefits installers but pressures manufacturers.

At the same time, policy-driven market segmentation is emerging, with the U.S. increasingly favoring domestic suppliers while European markets implement their own local preferences. This fragmentation creates opportunities for strategically positioned manufacturers like TOYO to capture premium pricing in protected markets.

Navigating this evolving landscape is crucial for a solar solutions company such as TOYO, whose geographic diversification allows it to tap into various growing markets.

This process of adaptation has not been without pain. For instance, in the second half of 2024, imports from Southeast Asia faced anti-dumping and countervailing duties investigations. Currently, the individual anti-dumping rate for Vietnam is between 54.46% and 271.28% and the preliminary countervailing duty rate is 2.85%. This effectively acts as a disincentive for importing Vietnamese solar solutions into the U.S. While management has redirected Vietnamese cell capacity to India and Taiwan, while meeting U.S. demand from Ethiopia, it is notable that India's solar cell market is also undergoing a supply glut, as anti-dumping and countervailing duty investigations lead manufacturers to India.

Policy Changes Could Upend U.S. Economics

TOYO's single biggest threat is a policy and trade regime shift that removes its structural advantage in the U.S. market. The U.S. is currently in a revolution regarding its trade policy, and with midterms this year, there is ample incentive for the government to accelerate changes in the event they lose the midterms. Moreover, currently, the Trump Administration is waiting for a ruling on the legality of tariffs. While the president has indicated that it will use other presidential powers to maintain the tariff level, the possibility that the U.S. plant can not only manufacture efficiently, but do so within a protected profit pool created by American industrial policy, may be hurt by the fall of tariffs. Nevertheless, U.S. industrial policy as a whole will remain, with its production-linked incentives, so many favorable protections will remain for U.S. based producers.

U.S. policy could change regarding manufacturing credits, which could be further narrowed, delayed, challenged, or rendered less valuable in practice, hurting TOYO's ability to improve its profitability. At the same time, TOYO's Ethiopian plant, currently positioned as a tariff-manageable, and an ant-dumping/countervailing duties-safe supply source into the U.S., is inherently vulnerable to reclassification risk. A broadening of anti-circumvention enforcement, extension of duties to new jurisdictions, or tightening what qualifies as "domestic" content could simultaneously impair TOYO's market access and compress realized pricing. This would force TOYO's operating model back into the global commodity arena, where oversupply and aggressive Chinese pricing set the clearing price, and where scale alone is not enough to defend margins. Unable to reallocate factories or quickly reconfigure as policy changes, this creates downside. Without a continuity of existing rules, or favourable policy changes, TOYO could suffer tremendously. That said, both Republicans and Democrats have largely embraced the idea of protecting domestic manufacturers and reshoring production, so while the type of policies that will exist at the end of the year or at the end of the Trump Administration, or the end of the succeeding administration, may be uncertain, neither party has a free market wing in the ascendancy that would push out industrial policy. With TOYO already through the door, the most catastrophic policy options are unlikely to affect them badly.

Valuation Offers Further Upside

At the current price, TOYO has a price/earnings multiple of 9.88, compared to TOYO seems undervalued compared to Seeking Alpha's 32.71 sector median, and Aswath Damodaran's sector median of 44.56. This seems to be a reflection of the market's skepticism around its ability to sustain its profitability. Historically, the company's profitability has swung wildly, making it hard to predict profits for the next year. Without that smoothness of earnings, analysts and investors have been cautious about making predictions on the company. The market has not properly accounted for the impact of the firm's integration into the U.S. market, and so, has not re-rated the firm for a period of more sustainable and higher profits. As a result, TOYO is grossly undervalued.
